title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Market Trends Strengthen as New Regulations Come into Force
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(6th January â€“ 12th January)
  • A total of 444,485 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 42.80% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades increased by 78.80% WoW as 2,013,973 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price decreased this week to $73.89/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording a 0.24%Â fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 1.41% in price, reaching $69.86/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price decreased by 1.51% WoW; On 14th Jan, the spot price stood at $74.63/MT. The benchmark price stood at $79.38/MT on 14th
  • D3 RIN prices increased by 3.72% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increase by 6.78% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The latest market data reflects a dynamic start to 2025, with trading volumes showing significant growth after the year-end clearing activity. Prices have experienced minor fluctuations, with some softening in weekly averages despite broader indicators of market strength. The rebound in Type 1 prices suggests continued confidence, likely influenced by regulatory developments. Meanwhile, RIN markets are seeing notable price increases across categories, underscoring the impact of evolving renewable fuel mandates and compliance strategies. The release of the Section 45V rule introduces both challenges and opportunities. The new framework establishes a tiered credit system with base credits ranging from $0.60/kg to $3.00/kg of hydrogen. Key challenges include restrictions on feedstock blending, raising cost-efficiency concerns for producers and environmental risks highlighted by advocacy groups. Standardized CI scoring for animal manure RNG and delayed book-and-claim accounting until 2027 may impact credit aggregation, though stacking 45V and LCFS credits remains permitted. On the policy front, Oregonâ€™s Clean Fuels Program has adopted a new lifecycle carbon intensity model, marking a shift that mirrors recent changes in Californiaâ€™s Low-Carbon Fuel Standards.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_591243" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 0.24% WoW fall, decreasing from $74.07 to $73.89 this week, with all non-zero credit prices decreased by 1.41% WoW, falling from $70.86 to $69.86. Type 1 trades saw 444,485 credits exchanged, reflecting a 42.80% WoW increase. Similarly, the total transaction volume for all non-zero trades increased by 78.80% WoW to reach 2,013,973 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_591244" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 1.51% WoW, reaching $74.30/MT. On 14th January, the average spot price was $74.63/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $79.38/MT as of 14th January.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_591245" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 140 trades were reported in the market, with 23 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $100.00 for six Type 2 transactions. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $47.50 per credit of Type 2, reported for three transactions at volumes of 3,000, 5,000 and 10,000 credits. The smallest volume transfers of a single credit of Type 2 category, were undertaken for price of $67.55 whereas the largest volume of transfer was reported at 80,000 MT of Type 2 category at $69.00 and $77.85.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_591246" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] In December 2024, trading volumes surged to 7.38M, a significant increase from 2.01M in November 2024, driven by year-end balance clearing. Average Type 1 prices in December were around $71.89, reflecting a slight dip from November's prices. However, trading activity in the first two weeks of January 2025 shows volumes of approximately 2.85M, with prices rebounding to an average of $73.57, indicating continued market strength.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_591247" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 6th Jan â€“ 12th Jan, total VPT recorded a 6.42% decrease (WoW), with all non-zero prices falling over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices increased by 3.72% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increase by 6.78% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • California Envisions a Zero-Emission, Fully Connected Rail Network by 2050. Governor Gavin Newsom announced the release of the State Rail Plan, a long-range vision of statewide investments that will create up to 900,000 jobs and a zero-emission, fully interconnected rail and transit network by 2050. The release of the plan comes after the Governor joined local leaders and workers yesterday to mark the beginning of the track-laying phase for California high-speed rail, which will serve as the backbone of the stateâ€™s rail network.
  • S. Department of the Treasury Releases Final Rules for Technology-Neutral Clean Electricity Credits. The U.S. Department of the Treasury and the IRS released final rules for the Clean Electricity Investment and Production Tax Credits â€“ also known as the technology-neutral credits â€“ in tax code sections 45Y and 48E. These credits are central to cutting energy costs for American families and businesses and producing abundant, affordable power to meet growing demand created by major investments in the U.S. economy. According to analysis from the Department of Energy, the tech-neutral credits, along with other Inflation Reduction Act and Bipartisan Infrastructure Law provisions, are expected to save American families up to $38 billion on electricity bills through 2030.
  • Aemetis Biogas Commissioned Three New Dairy Digesters in December 2024, Increasing Expected RNG Production Capacity by 80% to 550,000 MMBtu Per Year for 2025. Aemetis, Inc. (NasdaqGM: AMTX), a renewable natural gas and renewable fuels company, announced that its Aemetis Biogas subsidiary commissioned three new dairy digesters in December 2024, increasing from nine to a total of twelve operating digesters. The additional digesters are expected to generate 250,000 MMBtu per year of renewable natural gas (RNG), contributing to a planned 550,000 MMBtu of RNG production in 2025.
  • OPAL Fuels Signs 60 Million Gallon RNG Supply Agreement for Five New Fueling Stations Built by OPAL. OPAL Fuels Inc. announced it has executed a long-term agreement with a market-leading provider of integrated logistics solutions to supply renewable natural gas (â€œRNGâ€) at five new fueling stations that are currently being built and will be serviced by OPAL Fuels under existing long-term agreements. The six-year RNG supply agreement anticipates a combined annual volume of approximately 12 million gasoline gallon equivalent (GGE) once all stations are operational.
  • The Environmental Quality Commission Approved the Clean Fuels Program 2024 Rulemaking. The Environmental Quality Commission today approved the Clean Fuels Program 2024 rulemaking, which makes changes and updates to both the Clean Fuels Program and the Third Party Verification Programâ€™s rule divisions, along with adopting the OR-GREET 4.0 lifecycle fuel carbon intensity model to replace our current OR-GREET 3.0 model.
  • U.S. Department of the Treasury Releases Guidance on Clean Fuels Production Credit. The U.S. Department of the Treasury (Treasury) and Internal Revenue Service (IRS) have released guidance on the Clean Fuels Production Credit (section 45Z). Section 45Z provides a tax credit for the production of transportation fuels with lifecycle greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions below 50 kilograms of CO2e per mmBTU. The credit is in effect in 2025 and is for sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) and non-SAF transportation fuels produced after 31st December 2024 and sold before 31st December 2027.
